Taking plastic out of dentistry

Dentistry free from plastic.

Modern dentistry is overreliant on plastic — from the bonding on your teeth, to your retainer, to your toothbrush. Most of it sheds microplastics as it wears, and almost no one tests for it.

bio. exists to change that, one product at a time.

What bio. is

A dental brand with one job: remove the plastic.

The plastic dentistry relies on doesn't belong in your mouth — and no one has been held to account for what it sheds.

We're rebuilding dental care with natural, biocompatible materials. Retainers first. Then bonding, aligners, everyday wear.

The scale of it

Dental wear leaches plastic.

Dental devices break down with wear, shedding microplastics that build up in the body over time. The two that touch you most:

DeviceMade ofWhat wear releases
RetainerWorn nightly for yearsPET-G thermoplasticMicro- and nanoplastics, shed through years of abrasion and swallowed in saliva
Clear alignerWorn 20–22 hrs a dayPolyurethane copolymer5–20 µm particles under near-constant friction; the finest can cross the gut lining
The risks

What microplastics do to your body

Lungs

Airborne particles found deep in lung tissue, linked to airway inflammation.

Gut

Swallowed daily and recovered routinely from the gut; linked to inflammation.

Hormones

BPA and plasticisers shed during wear are recognised endocrine disruptors.

Pregnancy

Found in placental tissue; children and the unborn are most at risk.

Source: Shariff et al. (2025), Progress in Orthodontics 26(1), Article 49.

The bio. material difference

What bio. is made of.

Most brands never disclose their materials. We do the opposite — every substance disclosed, every claim tested, every result published.

Upload a digital scan

/ 03 — From existing models

Send physical models

Already have study models from a previous clinician? Post them to us and we'll digitise them to build your retainer. We return your originals with your finished set.

The bio. Standard

If we can't test it, we don't claim it.

Most dental devices are thermoplastic, and most makers have never published what they shed during wear. We consider that unacceptable.

The bio. Standard is our public testing protocol: every product independently tested by an ISO 17025-accredited lab, every result published by lot number and date.

What every product is tested for.

TestMethodStandardResult
Microplastic particle releaseSimulated wear (saliva simulant, 37°C, 14 days)ISO 10993-12Below detection limit
BPA, BPS, BPF leachateLC-MS/MS analysis of eluateEU Reg. (EC) No 10/2011Not detected
PhthalatesGC-MS extraction analysisREACH Annex XVIINot detected
Heavy metalsICP-MS (oral simulant)ISO 10993-13Below limits
BiocompatibilityCytotoxicity assayISO 10993-5Biocompatible
Polymer compositionFTIR spectroscopyIn-house disclosurePublished

Lab report PDFs are published here by lot number and test date. If we don't know something, we say so. If we can't support a claim, we don't make it.

How we operate

Three things we'll never compromise on

I

If we can't test it, we don't claim it.

Every material claim is backed by independent lab testing, filed, and linked publicly. When we don't know something, we say so.

II

Supervision is not optional.

bio. is not teledentistry. Every case is managed by a registered UK clinician. Clean materials and proper supervision are the same product.

III

Adult tone, always.

No adjectives without evidence. No fear marketing. Our customer knows more than most marketers assume — we write for them, not at them.

About bio.

A question nobody was asking.

If you care what's in your food, your skincare, your cleaning products — why would you not care about what's in the device you wear in your mouth for eight hours every night?

We built bio. to answer it.

Most retainers are standard thermoplastic, and most aligner brands have never published what their materials shed during wear. Nobody was even asking the question.

bio. retainers are the only ones in the UK independently tested for microplastic release and confirmed clean. Every claim on this site is backed by published data.

The parallel

The clean beauty movement reformed skincare over a decade. The dental device industry has never been held to the same standard. bio. is that brand for oral care.

FAQs

What's proven, and what isn't.

The honest separation between what the science supports and what's still being studied.

What are bio. retainers made of? +
A bio-derived, plant-based polymer — independently tested and confirmed free from harmful microplastics, BPA, BPS, BPF and phthalates. No other UK retainer brand has published equivalent testing. Full results are on The bio. Standard.
Why are bio. retainers the only ones free from microplastics? +
Every retainer in the UK market — including those from dental practices and other DTC brands — is made from standard thermoplastic that has not been independently tested for microplastic shedding. bio. is the first to test ours and the first to publish the data.
How does the impression kit work? +
Order online. Your kit arrives next day with step-by-step instructions and a video walkthrough. It takes about 10 minutes at home. Post it back in the included pre-paid packaging. Custom retainers are crafted and delivered within 5 working days.
Can I use an existing scan or model? +
Yes. If your dentist has an intraoral scan, upload the STL or PLY files directly. Or post physical study models and we'll digitise them in our lab — your originals are returned with your finished set.
Why do I need to replace my retainer each year? +
Teeth shift and materials age. An annual replacement is the standard of care, and the annual subscription keeps a fresh, tested set on your teeth without re-ordering.
Is this teledentistry? +
No. bio. is not unsupervised teledentistry. Every case is managed by a registered UK clinician. Clean materials and proper supervision are part of the same product.
Are aligners free from microplastics too? +
bio.aligners are in development and will use a bio-derived, independently tested material. The microplastic-free claim is specific to our retainer for now, because that is where exposure is highest — worn nightly, for years.
Where can I read the actual lab results? +
On The bio. Standard page, published by lot number and test date, and re-tested on every new production batch.

Legal · Last updated June 2026

Privacy Policy

This template covers the essentials. Have it reviewed by a UK solicitor or generated via a compliant tool (e.g. iubenda) before you take live orders.

Who we are

bio. is operated by Oravertis Ltd, a company registered in the United Kingdom. For any privacy question, contact hello@bio.uk. We are the data controller for the personal data described here.

What we collect

  • Contact and order details you provide — name, email, delivery address, and dental impression or scan data needed to make your retainer.
  • Payment information, processed securely by our payment provider (Stripe). We do not store your full card details.
  • Usage data — basic analytics about how the site is used, where you consent to it.

How we use it

  • To fulfil your order, manufacture your device, and provide customer support.
  • To manage your subscription and send service communications.
  • With your consent, to send marketing emails — which you can unsubscribe from at any time.

Sharing

We share data only with the providers needed to run the service — our payment processor, manufacturing and delivery partners, and email provider — and only as required. We never sell your data.

Your rights

Under UK GDPR you have the right to access, correct, delete, or restrict use of your personal data, and to lodge a complaint with the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO). To exercise any right, email hello@bio.uk.

Retention

We keep personal and clinical data only as long as needed to provide the service and meet legal and medical-record obligations, then delete or anonymise it.

This is a starting template, not legal advice. Confirm specifics (cookie use, retention periods, sub-processors) with a qualified adviser before launch.

Legal · Last updated June 2026

Terms & Conditions

This template covers the essentials. Have it reviewed by a UK solicitor before you take live orders.

The basics

These terms govern your purchase of bio. products from Oravertis Ltd. By placing an order you agree to them. Please read them alongside our Privacy Policy.

Orders and payment

  • Prices are shown in GBP and include VAT where applicable. Payment is taken securely at checkout via Stripe.
  • A one-off set is a single purchase. The annual subscription renews every 12 months until cancelled.
  • You can cancel a subscription at any time by emailing hello@bio.uk before your next renewal date.

Custom-made products

Retainers are custom-made to your impressions. Because they are made to order and personalised, your statutory right to cancel may be limited once manufacture has begun — except where a product is faulty or not as described. Your legal rights under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 are unaffected.

Clinical responsibility

bio. devices are provided under the oversight of a registered UK clinician. They are not a substitute for in-person dental care where that is clinically required. If you have an active dental health issue, see your dentist.

Delivery

We aim to deliver custom retainers within 5 working days of receiving usable impressions or scans. Timeframes are estimates, not guarantees.

Liability

Nothing in these terms limits liability where it would be unlawful to do so. Otherwise our liability is limited to the value of your order.

This is a starting template, not legal advice. A UK solicitor should review these terms — particularly the cancellation and clinical-responsibility sections — before you take orders.

Materials · 6 min read

What are dental retainers made of?

Almost every retainer sold in the UK is made from one of a handful of thermoplastics — and almost no manufacturer publishes what those materials release during years of nightly wear. Here is what they actually are.

If you've finished orthodontic treatment, you've almost certainly been handed a retainer and told to wear it nightly, indefinitely. What you were probably not told is what it's made of. For a device that sits against your teeth and gums for eight hours a night, often for decades, that's a surprising gap — and it's the gap this article exists to close.

The two main types of retainer

Retainers fall broadly into two categories, and the material story is different for each.

Clear (vacuum-formed) retainers

These are the transparent, removable retainers most people picture — the same style as a clear aligner, but worn to hold teeth in place rather than move them. They are almost always made from a thermoplastic, most commonly polyethylene terephthalate glycol (PET-G), or sometimes polypropylene or a polyurethane-based copolymer. The plastic is heated and vacuum-formed over a model of your teeth.

Bonded (fixed) retainers

A bonded retainer is a thin wire fixed to the back of the front teeth with composite resin. The wire is usually stainless steel or a nickel-titanium alloy; the resin that holds it is a BPA-derived composite — the same broad family of materials used in white fillings.

What thermoplastics shed during wear

Plastics are not inert. Under mechanical stress, temperature change and contact with saliva, thermoplastics degrade at the surface and release particles. In the context of a retainer, that means micro- and nanoplastic particles, and in some cases chemical additives, can be released into the mouth and swallowed.

This isn't fringe science. Peer-reviewed studies have measured particle and chemical release from orthodontic thermoplastics under simulated oral conditions, and a 2025 narrative review in Progress in Orthodontics drew the existing literature together, concluding that leaching of microplastics and associated compounds from clinical dental polymers is a real and under-examined exposure pathway.

The retainer is, for most people, the single longest-contact plastic device they will ever put in their body — worn nightly, for years, against soft tissue and in constant contact with saliva. Yet it is one of the least scrutinised.

Why don't manufacturers publish testing data?

Because they aren't required to, and because testing costs money and creates a paper trail. Medical-device regulations focus on biocompatibility — broadly, "does this provoke an adverse biological reaction" — rather than long-term microplastic shedding, which is a newer concern that existing standards weren't written to address. The result is that you can buy a retainer from a high-street practice or an online brand and find no published data anywhere on what it releases during wear.

What to look for

  • Ask what polymer your retainer is made from. A brand that knows — and will tell you — is already ahead of most.
  • Ask whether the material has been independently tested for microplastic release, not just biocompatibility.
  • Ask whether any testing results are published, by batch or lot, rather than simply asserted.
This is the question bio. was built to answer. Our retainers are made from a bio-derived polymer and independently tested, lot by lot, for microplastic particle release, BPA, BPS, BPF and phthalates — with every result published on The bio. Standard.

Source: Shariff et al. (2025), "Microplastics and nanoplastics in clinical dentistry and orthodontics," Progress in Orthodontics 26(1), Article 49.

Microplastics and clear aligners: what the research says → How often should you replace your retainer? →
Research · 8 min read

Microplastics and clear aligners: what the research says

Clear aligners are worn 20–22 hours a day. A growing body of peer-reviewed work has begun measuring what that constant wear releases. Here is a plain-English summary of the evidence — and the honest limits of it.

Clear aligners transformed orthodontics by making tooth movement nearly invisible. But their defining feature — that they're worn almost continuously — also makes them the highest-exposure plastic device in routine dental use. A tray spends the day under friction from chewing, speaking and grinding, bathed in warm saliva, before being swapped for the next in the series. Researchers have started asking what that does at the material's surface.

What studies have measured

Several laboratory studies have placed orthodontic aligner and retainer materials into simulated oral environments — artificial saliva at body temperature, sometimes with mechanical cycling to mimic wear — and then measured what leaches out. The findings, broadly, are consistent:

  • Thermoplastic aligner materials release microplastic and nanoplastic particles under simulated wear, with release increasing as mechanical stress and time increase.
  • Some materials release measurable quantities of chemical additives, including bisphenols (such as BPA) and other compounds used in plastic manufacture.
  • Particle release is not uniform — it varies by material, by brand, and by how the tray is handled, cleaned and worn.

The 2025 narrative review in Progress in Orthodontics synthesised this work and concluded that leaching from clinical dental and orthodontic polymers is a genuine exposure route that deserves far more attention than it currently receives.

What we know about the health side

This is where honesty matters. The materials science — that plastics shed particles and additives during wear — is increasingly well established. The downstream health picture is less settled, because it's a young field.

What is known from the broader microplastics literature: particles have been found in human lung tissue, in the gut, in blood and even in placental tissue, and certain plastic additives such as bisphenols are recognised endocrine disruptors. What is not yet known with precision is the dose-response relationship for dental-device exposure specifically — how much reaches the body, and what threshold, if any, produces harm.

Our position is to state both halves plainly. The release is measurable and documented. The long-term health consequences of that specific exposure are still being studied. We don't think uncertainty about the second half is a reason to ignore the first.

Why retainers are the sharper concern than aligners

It may seem counterintuitive, given aligners are worn more hours per day. But aligners are replaced every one to two weeks as you progress through a series, so any single tray's contact time is short. A retainer, by contrast, is worn nightly for years — sometimes decades — and is often never tested at all. Cumulative contact time is the variable that matters, and on that measure the humble retainer is the bigger exposure story.

What you can reasonably do

  • Choose materials that have been independently tested for particle release, not just regulatory biocompatibility.
  • Replace worn devices on schedule — a degraded, scratched tray sheds more than a fresh one.
  • Avoid hot liquids while wearing trays, which accelerates material breakdown.
  • Favour brands that publish their data rather than assert their claims.
bio. is building plastic-free, independently tested dental devices — retainers first, with aligners in development. Every material is tested for microplastic release and the results are published on The bio. Standard.

Source: Shariff et al. (2025), Progress in Orthodontics 26(1), Article 49, and the wider peer-reviewed literature on microplastic leaching from dental polymers.

Guides · 7 min read

Invisalign alternatives in the UK: an honest guide

Invisalign defined the clear-aligner category, but it's no longer the only option in the UK. From at-home brands to clinic-led treatment, here's a clear-eyed look at the alternatives — and what to scrutinise before you commit.

"Invisalign" has become shorthand for clear aligners the way "Hoover" became shorthand for vacuum cleaners. But the UK market now holds a wide spread of alternatives at very different price points and clinical models. Choosing well means understanding what actually varies between them — and it's not just price.

The three broad models

1. Clinic-led aligners

Traditional and still the gold standard for complex cases: you're treated in-practice by a dentist or specialist orthodontist who takes scans, plans the movement and monitors progress in person. Highest oversight, highest cost, least convenient.

2. At-home / direct-to-consumer aligners

Brands that post you an impression kit, plan remotely and ship trays to your door with little or no in-person supervision. Cheapest and most convenient — but oversight varies enormously, and the collapse of SmileDirectClub in 2023 put a spotlight on the risks of minimal clinical involvement.

3. Hybrid models

A middle path: one or two in-person appointments for scanning and clinical checks, with treatment planned by a qualified clinician and the rest handled remotely. Aims to keep convenience without abandoning supervision.

What to actually compare

Price is the obvious axis, but it's rarely the one that determines outcome. The questions that matter more:

  • Who plans your treatment? A specialist orthodontist, a general dentist, or software? Tooth movement is a clinical act, and who designs it matters.
  • Is there real clinical oversight? Is a registered clinician responsible for your case, or are you essentially self-treating?
  • What happens if something goes wrong? Relapse, pain, a tooth that won't move — is there a named person accountable?
  • What is the device made of? Almost nobody asks this. Aligners and retainers are plastic worn against your teeth for months or years, and material disclosure is vanishingly rare.
  • What's the retention plan? Straightening is only half the job. Without a good retainer, teeth drift back — so the retainer's quality and material matter as much as the aligners.
The lesson of the DTC aligner shake-out wasn't "avoid convenience." It was "don't accept the removal of clinical oversight as the price of it." The strongest alternatives keep a qualified clinician genuinely responsible for your case.

Where bio. fits

bio. is building a hybrid, clinician-supervised model with one distinguishing feature no other UK brand offers: independently tested, plastic-free materials with published results. Retainers are available now; aligners are in development. The principle across both is the same — convenience without sacrificing either supervision or material safety.

Start with the device you'll wear longest. bio. retainers are the only UK retainers independently tested for microplastic release and confirmed clean — results on The bio. Standard.

Care · 5 min read

How often should you replace your retainer?

Most people wear the same retainer for far longer than they should. Retainers warp, wear thin and accumulate bacteria — and as the plastic degrades, it sheds more. Here's what the evidence suggests about timing.

A retainer feels like a buy-once object. In practice it's a consumable — a precision plastic device that degrades with use. Wearing a worn-out retainer isn't just less effective at holding your teeth; as the surface breaks down, it sheds more particles and harbours more bacteria. So "how often should I replace it?" is a real question with a real answer.

Why retainers wear out

  • Mechanical fatigue. Nightly insertion, removal and grinding gradually warp and thin the plastic, loosening the fit until it no longer holds teeth precisely.
  • Material degradation. Repeated exposure to saliva, temperature change and cleaning agents breaks down the polymer surface over time — and a degraded surface sheds more microplastic particles than a fresh one.
  • Biofilm and staining. Retainers accumulate bacterial biofilm and calcium deposits. Beyond hygiene, a roughened, colonised surface is harder to clean and more prone to further breakdown.

The general guidance

For clear (vacuum-formed) retainers, many clinicians suggest replacement roughly every 6 to 12 months with regular wear, though this varies with how heavily you grind, how you clean it, and the specific material. Bonded retainers last longer but should be checked at routine dental visits for breakages and the gum health around them. The honest answer is that "until it visibly breaks" is the wrong threshold — performance and material integrity decline well before failure is obvious.

This is precisely why bio. retainers are offered on an annual subscription. A fresh, independently tested set arrives each year, so you're never wearing a degraded device — and never wearing one whose material was never tested in the first place.

How to make a retainer last as long as it should

  • Rinse and clean it daily with a non-abrasive cleaner — never hot water, which warps the plastic.
  • Store it dry in a ventilated case, not wrapped in tissue where it gets lost or crushed.
  • Keep it away from heat — windowsills, dashboards, radiators all deform thermoplastic.
  • Replace on schedule rather than waiting for visible damage.
bio. retainers are made from a bio-derived polymer, independently tested for microplastic release, and replaced annually by subscription. Results published on The bio. Standard.
